skill.name: refactor
skill.description: Guides safe, incremental code refactoring that improves maintainability without changing behavior. It covers common code smells, function extraction, type safety, design patterns, and review checklists.
skill.summary: Improve existing code structure through small, behavior-preserving refactors.
skill.author: github

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many high and medium patterns, but review found they are false positives from Markdown fences, template literals, prose, and illustrative code examples. No executable scripts, command invocation instructions, network activity, credential access, prompt injection, or data exfiltration behavior was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences and JavaScript template literals used in documentation examples. They are not executable skill scripts and do not instruct the agent to run shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is visible as Markdown fences or JavaScript interpolation inside examples. No shell command, Ruby backtick execution, or runnable helper file is present.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Weak Crypto Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts correspond to ordinary words such as description, design, and descriptive. No MD5, SHA-1, DES, cryptographic API, hashing code, or security-sensitive algorithm choice appears at these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of each cited line shows normal documentation text, not cryptographic implementation. The pattern appears to be a substring match rather than a semantic finding.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Reconnaissance Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The system and network reconnaissance alerts point to validation examples, a safe refactoring checklist, and process guidance. The skill does not mention or invoke host discovery, port scanning, interface enumeration, or network probing tools.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are benign text or application validation examples. No reconnaissance command names, network targets, or collection workflow were found.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positive: Hidden File Access
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hidden file alert points to a regular expression inside an email validation example. It is not a filesystem path and does not access hidden files or directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains an email format regular expression in documentation. There is no file path, dotfile name, filesystem API, or instruction to read hidden files.

content.value_statement: Large or tangled code slows every change and makes defects harder to isolate. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through small refactors that preserve behavior.
actual_capabilities: Identifies common code smells such as long functions, duplication, large classes, and magic values.
Suggests behavior-preserving refactors, including extraction, renaming, guard clauses, and parameter objects.
Guides type safety improvements for untyped or loosely typed interfaces.
Maps selected design patterns to specific refactoring problems.
Provides a safe refactoring process with testing, review, and cleanup checkpoints.
limitations: It is guidance content, not an automated refactoring engine.
Behavior preservation still depends on tests and reviewer validation.
Most examples use TypeScript or JavaScript style syntax.
It is not intended for full rewrites or broad architecture replacement.

use_cases.title: Clean Up a Difficult Module
use_cases.description: Break a hard-to-read module into smaller functions while keeping current behavior stable.

use_cases.title: Reduce Review Risk Before a Feature
use_cases.description: Plan small preparatory refactors before adding a feature to fragile code.

use_cases.title: Improve Legacy Code Gradually
use_cases.description: Use checklists and examples to reduce duplication, clarify names, and improve types over time.

prompt_templates.title: Find Refactoring Targets
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner scan of one file or module.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this file for code smells. List the safest refactoring opportunities and explain the expected benefit of each one.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Small Refactor
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn a general cleanup request into ordered steps.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a behavior-preserving refactoring plan for this module. Keep each step small and include tests to run after each step.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Focused Functions
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve a long function without changing behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this long function by extracting focused helpers. Preserve public behavior and explain how each helper improves readability.

prompt_templates.title: Apply a Design Pattern Carefully
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced cleanup of repeated conditionals or responsibilities.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether a design pattern would simplify this code. Apply it only if it reduces complexity, and describe the tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: A single function fetches data, validates it, calculates totals, updates state, and sends notifications.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend extracting separate helpers for fetching, validation, pricing, state updates, and notifications. It would keep the public function as the orchestration point.

output_examples.input: Two services contain repeated discount rules with different variable names.
output_examples.output: The skill would identify duplication, propose one shared discount rule function, and recommend tests that compare old and new results.

output_examples.input: A module uses many loose objects and nullable values without clear contracts.
output_examples.output: The skill would suggest typed interfaces, guard clauses, and explicit result handling before larger structural changes.

best_practices: Start with tests or add characterization tests before changing structure.
Make one small refactor at a time and verify behavior after each step.
Separate refactoring commits from feature changes so reviews stay focused.
anti_patterns: Do not combine broad rewrites with refactoring requests.
Do not refactor critical production paths without tests or a rollback plan.
Do not introduce design patterns unless they reduce real complexity.

faq.question: Does this skill change application behavior?
faq.answer: Its guidance is designed to preserve behavior. Tests and review are still required to confirm the result.

faq.question: Can it refactor an entire repository at once?
faq.answer: It is best for gradual improvements to focused files or modules. Large rewrites need a different workflow.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: The concepts are general, but the examples are mostly TypeScript and JavaScript style.

faq.question: Does it run external tools?
faq.answer: No executable tool is included. It recommends tests and review steps that the user or agent may run separately.

faq.question: How should I start with risky legacy code?
faq.answer: Add characterization tests first, then make the smallest useful refactor and verify behavior before continuing.

faq.question: Can it help with type safety?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for introducing interfaces, domain types, nullable handling, and clearer public contracts.
